metric_value: Centralize each data center's storage on SANs supplemented with NAS
notes: Step 1 of 6-step ILM adoption recipe; networked storage enables centrally managed pools with greatest management economies

metric_value: Create pools of storage on three axes: data type and structure and use frequency and storage tier
notes: Step 2: classify data by structured/semi-structured/unstructured type and active/inactive frequency and online/midline/nearline/offline tier

metric_value: Create lifecycle policies starting with backup/restore then adding ILM-oriented hierarchy-based policies
notes: Step 3: improve storage utilization and reduce admin costs; determine compliance and data disposal best practices; involves legal and other stakeholders

metric_value: Populate new/rehosted applications on appropriate pools at five-year refresh points
notes: Step 4: every application platform changes within five years; develop standards mandating pool placement for new/rehosted platforms

metric_value: Drive economies of scale through virtualization and automation with rising TB-per-admin ratio
notes: Step 5: ILM policies become more sophisticated; midline pool absorbs much of what was on expensive FC/SCSI disks

metric_value: Implement intelligent ILM-based storage when cross-application policy management software matures circa 2008-2010
notes: Step 6: expect 80% reduction in storage-related hours on production systems; requires legal and audit compliance sign-off

metric_value: Affordable now for even small enterprises
notes: SANs positioned as foundational infrastructure for ILM; mature enough for broad enterprise adoption by 2004

metric_value: Supplement to SANs for file-level storage in ILM architecture
notes: NAS described as complement to SAN-based block storage; together they enable centralized networked storage

metric_value: Online dynamic tier for OLTP and high-activity DSS on structured database data
notes: Highest-performance and most expensive storage tier; growth should be modest as midline absorbs workloads

metric_value: 25% of the cost of FC/SCSI for moderate random access on seldom-changing data
notes: Capacity-oriented disks for active fixed content and compliance data; more than half of enterprise data is semi-structured or unstructured and seldom changing

metric_value: Compresses backup times by up to 50% compared to disk-to-tape
notes: Disk library positioned as successor to tape library for backup/restore; nearline buffered tier adds midline disk-to-disk capability

metric_value: Offline sequential storage for disaster recovery with significant litigation liability
notes: No change in offsite tape for DR but tapes represent enormous litigation liability; data retention policy adherence critical

metric_value: Cross-application ILM management software is four to five years away (circa 2008-2009)
notes: Full enterprise-wide integrated ILM requires cross-application policy management software not yet available in 2004

metric_value: 45% per year
notes: Industry projection of storage growth rate driving huge swing toward putting reference data on midline disks

metric_value: 25% of FC/SCSI cost
notes: Midline disks at roughly one-quarter the cost per GB of Fibre Channel/SCSI for moderate random access workloads

metric_value: 80% reduction in storage-related hours on production systems by 2008-2010
notes: Expected productivity gain from mature ILM-based policy management; more storage overall due to growth but dramatically less admin effort

metric_value: Online dynamic (FC/SCSI) plus midline (capacity disks) plus nearline buffered (D2D backup) plus offline (tape DR)
notes: Four-pool storage model standardizing on four data storage types most often found in the enterprise; increasing adoption among IT professionals

metric_value: Three axes: data type (structured/semi-structured/unstructured) and use frequency (active/inactive and fixed/changeable) and storage tier (online/midline/nearline/offline)
notes: Systematic data classification framework matching application data requirements to appropriate storage pools

metric_value: Policy-driven management of information as it changes value throughout its lifecycle with data migrating around storage hierarchy based on enterprise policy
notes: Core ILM definition: generates better operational practices and consistent compliance and more economical storage use

metric_value: Cross-application ILM software matures circa 2008-2010
notes: Predicted four to five years from 2004 for practical enterprise-wide ILM; full legal and audit compliance sign-off not before this time

metric_value: Midline pool will absorb much of what was on expensive FC/SCSI disks
notes: Predicted at step 5 that growth in FC/SCSI should be modest as midline takes over capacity workloads

metric_value: Disk library is the successor to today's tape library for backup/restore
notes: Predicted disk-to-disk backup would replace tape-based backup; nearline buffered tier positioned as evolution of tape backup

metric_value: Virtualization and automation will drive rising TB-per-admin ratio
notes: Predicted that embedded virtualization and automation would reduce problem intervention demands on storage personnel

metric_value: ILM concepts became standard in enterprise storage management by 2008-2010 as predicted
notes: EMC and IBM and others delivered tiered storage management platforms; Symantec Enterprise Vault and similar products matured on timeline predicted

metric_value: Cloud storage tiers (hot/warm/cool/archive) validated the four-pool model
notes: AWS S3 tiers and Azure Blob tiers and Google Cloud Storage classes map directly to the four-pool model: Standard=online and Infrequent=midline and Glacier=nearline and Deep Archive=offline

metric_value: Tape persists for cold storage and compliance despite disk-to-disk backup dominance
notes: LTO tape continues for archival and compliance use cases; tape did not disappear as backup medium shifted to disk and cloud but retained niche role for cold/offline storage

metric_value: Storage automation and virtualization dramatically reduced admin effort as predicted
notes: Software-defined storage and cloud managed services validated the prediction; TB-per-admin ratios increased by orders of magnitude through automation

metric_value: Capacity tier became dominant storage pool absorbing expensive disk workloads
notes: SATA and NL-SAS and object storage became the dominant enterprise storage media by volume; flash/SSD replaced FC/SCSI for performance tier making the capacity tier even more dominant

metric_value: Offline tapes represent enormous litigation liability; data retention policy must be strictly adhered to
notes: Kastner warned about legal exposure from retained backup tapes; prescient given subsequent e-discovery regulations and litigation holds

metric_value: Waiting for ILM maturity is a poor approach; time should be used productively to evolve into ILM environment
notes: Central thesis: organizations should take incremental steps now rather than waiting for complete ILM software solutions

metric_value: More than half of enterprise data is semi-structured or unstructured and seldom changing
notes: Justification for midline storage tier adoption; this ratio has only increased with growth in unstructured data from digital content and IoT
